title: US–Iran Ceasefire Expires; Vance and Witkoff Fly to Islamabad as Iran Delegation Arrives
detail: The 14-day US–Iran ceasefire, brokered by Pakistan and agreed on April 7–8, 2026, expires on the evening of April 21. VP JD Vance and Special Envoy Steve Witkoff depart Washington for Islamabad for a second round of talks, despite Iran having publicly declared on April 20 it had 'no plans' to attend. In a last-minute reversal, Iranian sources confirmed to CNN that an Iranian delegation did arrive in Pakistan — raising cautious hopes that talks could resume. President Trump sharply raised the stakes, stating he was 'ready to go back to war' and expected to resume military operations if no agreement was reached. Key unresolved sticking points include the US demand that Iran freeze uranium enrichment and surrender its highly enriched uranium stockpile, and Iran's demand for an end to the US naval blockade and guarantees concerning Hezbollah. The US seizure of an Iranian-flagged cargo vessel in the Strait of Hormuz over the previous weekend had further complicated the diplomatic atmosphere. China's President Xi Jinping had called Saudi Crown Prince MBS on April 20 urging an 'immediate and comprehensive ceasefire,' highlighting broad international pressure on both parties. The episode illustrates the structural paradox of coercive diplomacy: threats capable of bringing parties to the table can simultaneously foreclose the trust necessary for agreement.

media.caption: Pakistani security personnel at a checkpoint in Islamabad ahead of the US–Iran second-round talks as the ceasefire deadline expires
US and Iran exchange threats as the fragile ceasefire approaches its expiration deadline

title: DRC–M23 Ceasefire Verification Mechanism Launches Initial Surveillance Missions
detail: Following the April 18 US State Department joint statement confirming operationalization of the Expanded Joint Verification Mechanism Plus (EJVM+), the Ceasefire Oversight and Verification Mechanism (COVM) begins initial verification missions in eastern DRC around April 21, within the agreed one-week window. The mechanism — involving Qatar, the United States, Switzerland, Togo, and the African Union — is designed to monitor compliance with the Doha Framework Agreement (signed November 15, 2025) and the Montreux process meetings of April 13–17, 2026. Parties also committed to complete prisoner releases within 10 days (by approximately April 28). Qatar, Switzerland, and the US serve as international guarantors of the COVM process. Despite the framework progress, fighting was reported to continue in highland areas of South Kivu, underlining the gap between ceasefire architecture and ground-level compliance. DRC government and AFC/M23 representatives had committed in the joint statement to protect civilians and allow humanitarian aid deliveries.

media.caption: DRC government and M23 rebels commit to protecting civilians and aid deliveries under the Doha Framework ceasefire
